channel.title: Business | The Guardian
channel.description: Latest financial, market & economic news and analysis from The Guardian
channel.copyright: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026
channel.dc:rights: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026

item.title: Oil price rises above $95 mark as Middle East conflict escalates
item.description:
Global supply disrupted by US-Iran aggression in strait of Hormuz and Houthi threats in Bab el-Mandeb

  • Middle East crisis live – latest updates

The price of oil has breached the $95 a barrel mark for the first time in six weeks as the escalating Middle East conflict threatens further disruption to global supplies.

The benchmark oil price rose sharply on Wednesday as renewed US-Iran aggression over the strait of Hormuz was compounded by Houthi threats to target vessels carrying Saudi oil through the Bab el-Mandeb strait.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jillian Ambrose Energy correspondent

item.title: Streeting says Burnham government will prioritise military over other spending
item.description:
Defence secretary says he will give arms industry ‘clarity and confidence’ in contrast to row preceding Starmer’s exit

Wes Streeting has said increased defence spending would make “an enormous contribution” to economic growth as he emphasised that the military will be a spending priority for Andy Burnham’s government over other departments.

The new defence secretary, in a speech at the Farnborough airshow, did not make any extra financial commitments but promised he would give the arms industry “clarity and confidence” in contrast to the row that contributed to the fall of Keir Starmer.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Dan Sabbagh Defence and security editor

item.title: Amazon founder Jeff Bezos in talks to join consortium seeking 30% stake in Liverpool
item.description:
• Billionaire keen to be part of Amit ­Bhatia’s £1.35bn bid

  • Bezos has previously explored buying NFL franchises

The Amazon founder Jeff Bezos has held talks about joining the consortium that is seeking to buy around 30% of Liverpool.

The group led by the former Queens Park Rangers co‑owner Amit Bhatia has made a provisional offer of £1.35bn to buy a stake from Fenway Sports Group (FSG), and is talking to other potential investors over supplying funding.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Matt Hughes and Will Unwin

item.title: Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlement
item.description:
Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work

The publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary of a $1.5bn (£1.12bn) copyright settlement between the AI startup Anthropic and thousands of authors over the use of their protected work to power chatbots.

Bloomsbury, which is home to the bestselling novelists Sarah J Maas and Susanna Clarke as well as JK Rowling, said it had 14,087 titles listed within the settlement, with a proposed compensation of about $3,000 a title.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Dan Milmo and Mark Sweney

item.title: UK inflation falls by more than expected to 2.6% in lift for Andy Burnham
item.description:
Annual June figure comes after new PM pledges to reduce cost of living and improve economy

UK inflation dropped by more than expected in June to 2.6%, in a boost for Andy Burnham’s plans to reduce the cost of living.

The new prime minister has pledged to bring down the cost of living to give households more disposable income and improve the outlook for the economy, announcing a winter VAT cut on electricity bills and lowering the cap on bus fares in England.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Phillip Inman

item.title: Lloyd’s of London says ex-boss’s ‘close relationship’ breached compliance rules
item.description:
John Neal failed to disclose ties with colleague that created a potential conflict of interest, investigation finds

Lloyd’s of London has ruled that its former boss John Neal breached compliance rules by failing to disclose a “close relationship” with a female colleague that risked triggering conflict of interest concerns at the insurance market.

An investigation by the Council of Lloyd’s, which is responsible for the market’s management and supervision, said it could not find any “conclusive evidence” Neal was romantically involved with the company’s then corporate affairs director, nor that there were any process failures relating to her promotion during Neal’s tenure.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kalyeena Makortoff Banking correspondent

item.title: Food price fears after Europe’s heatwave destroys estimated €2bn of crops
item.description:
Record June temperatures expected to cut grain harvest by 9m tonnes to its lowest level since 2018

The heatwave that swept across Europe in June is estimated to have cost the continent’s grain farmers €2bn (£1.7bn) in lost revenue and destroyed 9m tonnes of crops, sparking concern about the impact on the price of food.

Coceral, the European cereals and oilseeds trade association, made the estimate for the harvest of crops such as wheat, barley, maize and oats following the heatwave.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Joanna Partridge

item.title: Wetherspoon issues fourth profit warning in seven months
item.description:
Pub chain blames worse-than-expected sales on rising costs for workers, food, energy and property taxes

JD Wetherspoon has issued its fourth profit warning in seven months, with the pub chain blaming worse-than-expected sales, despite the World Cup, as it struggled with rising costs for food, workers, energy and property taxes.

Shares in the pub chain tumbled as much as 10% on Wednesday as its chair, Tim Martin, said profits would fall short of forecasts when it reports full-year earnings in October.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kalyeena Makortoff

item.title: Burnham announces £2 bus fare cap across England from January
item.description:
Measure to cost more than £500m, with money allocated to allow devolved nations to do the same

Single bus fares across England will be capped at £2 from January, Andy Burnham has announced, as he continues to introduce measures to tackle the cost of living in his first week as prime minister.

The nationwide cap is currently set at £3 until the end of March 2027, although some areas – including London and Greater Manchester, where Burnham set a £2 limit as mayor – have lower top rates for single tickets.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Topping Political correspondent

item.title: The Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challenged | Larry Elliott
item.description:
Fifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changed

Some things never change. Fifty years ago, Britain was sweltering in the long hot summer of 1976. The Labour government was acutely aware that the financial markets were scrutinising its every move. And a man called Healey was running the Treasury.

On the surface, the challenges facing John Healey are less severe than those Denis Healey had to grapple with half a century ago. Inflation is a lot lower and the City has reacted calmly to Andy Burnham as prime minister. And, while things could turn nasty pretty quickly, they would have to get a lot worse before Healey Mk II felt the need to ask the International Monetary Fund for an emergency loan.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Larry Elliott

item.title: Burnham has forced greater clarity at Thames Water. Now he must choose
item.description:
Bondholders are suddenly showing flexibility after 18 wasted months at ailing utility company

  • Thames Water investors offer ‘golden share’ in bid to head off nationalisation

What a difference a credible political threat of special administration at Thames Water can make. Suddenly, the bondholders are volunteering all manner of new ideas in their attempt to take over the ailing utility. Would you like the state to have a “golden share”, Mr Burnham? Here is a menu of “supervisory structures” that may meet your definition of “greater public control”. And please note that other “material improvements” to our last offer are on the way. Tell us what you want.

The bondholders’ sudden flexibility demonstrates the weakness in the government’s approach under Keir Starmer. The Treasury never hid its preference for a “market-based solution”, in the jargon. The result was a series of weak proposals from the bondholders – starting a year ago with an inadequate 20% haircut for them – that didn’t match the depth of the financial crisis at failing Thames. Now, at last, we may be cutting to the chase. We should have arrived at this point at least 18 months ago.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nils Pratley

item.title: Where will Andy Burnham find the money to fund his spending pledges?
item.description:
The new PM is already under pressure to explain how he will pay for his ambitious plans to remake Britain

Andy Burnham has made lowering the cost of living his first major policy announcement in government, with an immediate tax cut to remove VAT on energy bills.

As he begins to flesh out his agenda for government, the prime minister said the measure would provide “breathing space” for households. But Burnham is under close scrutiny over how his plans will be funded.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Richard Partington Senior economics correspondent

item.title: Fiscal hawk or big spender – what kind of chancellor will John Healey be?
item.description:
Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promises

On the frequent occasions that John Healey would travel abroad as defence secretary, he was so determined to keep working he would get the crew to tether a desk to the inside of his military aircraft so he could read his papers.

His diligence is one reason why Andy Burnham, the new prime minister, made Healey his unexpected pick for chancellor on Monday. Allies say others include their shared commitment to re-industrialisation and devolution.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kiran Stacey Policy editor

item.title: What will Andy Burnham’s VAT cut on electricity bills mean for you?
item.description:
New prime minister is implementing immediate tax cut to help ease cost of living pressures for British households

  • Andy Burnham to cut VAT on electricity bills as first cost of living move

Household electricity bills for people in Great Britain will not attract VAT for six months, under the first of a series of cost of living measures announced by the new prime minister, Andy Burnham.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hilary Osborne and Jillian Ambrose

item.title: Andy Burnham promised urgent action on the cost of living – the heat is now on chancellor John Healey to deliver it | Alfie Stirling
item.description:
The PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have one

  • Alfie Stirling is an economist and director of policy at the Joseph Rowntree Foundation

As Keir Starmer found, it takes more than words alone to warm a tepid bath. Voters reserve the greatest punishment for the politicians they think acted too little, or too late, to prevent them getting poorer. For Andy Burnham, much will now depend on whether he has got the right ideas, and in John Healey the right chancellor, to address Britain’s growing affordability crisis: not least as the new prime minister sets out new measures today to give people “some breathing space”.

Before Rachel Reeves took office, the average family was set to be worse off in 2029 than they had been in 2019. That’s not supposed to happen. The norm is for household incomes to rise by about £5,000 (2026 prices) every five years. Never before on modern record have they fallen across an entire decade.

Alfie Stirling is an economist and director of policy at the Joseph Rowntree Foundation

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Alfie Stirling

item.title: What the EU wants from Andy Burnham
item.description:
European leaders have made positive noises about the new UK PM, but he is likely to face the same restrictions as Starmer

Andy Burnham is unknown in Brussels, but that will soon change. In his schedule for the autumn is an EU-UK summit that may underscore the same dilemmas that have confronted previous British prime ministers in the relationship with Europe.

EU leaders were quick to offer congratulations to Britain’s prime minister, the seventh since the 2016 Brexit referendum. The European Commission president, Ursula von der Leyen, said she looked forward to strengthening the EU-UK partnership “for the security of our shared continent” and “the prosperity of people on both sides of the Channel”. The German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, invited Burnham to visit Berlin “very soon”, noting that the UK and Germany’s “deep connection is particularly evident in these times”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Rankin in Brussels and Lisa O’Carroll in London

item.title: Miliband has been relentlessly attacked for net zero; what will happen now?
item.description:
Tories, Reform, fossil fuel industry and some in his party lined up against decarbonisation target as energy secretary

“Whenever the Labour party feels a bit sad,” according to one close observer of the current government, speaking some months ago, “they wheel out Ed Miliband to talk about rooftop solar.”

It might not have been literally true, but it often felt during Keir Starmer’s premiership as if energy secretary Miliband brought the sun. Unfailingly upbeat, with a simple message that was understandable to voters, he had a clear mission – to decarbonise the UK’s power sector by 2030 – that was seen to be progressing fast.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Fiona Harvey Environment editor

item.title: Ex-Southern Water boss to face trial over allegedly manipulating sewage testing
item.description:
Former CEO Matthew Wright and three other ex-employees named in criminal proceedings by Environment Agency

The former chief executive of Southern Water is being prosecuted for conspiracy to defraud along with three other former employees, over allegations they manipulated sewage wastewater testing results to avoid tens of millions of pounds in fines.

Matthew Wright is the first water industry boss to face a criminal charge over sewage discharges. He and three others are charged with conspiracy to defraud the Environment Agency and ​the water regulator, Ofwat, between 2012 and 2017 by ⁠creating artificial “no-flow” ​events ​at wastewater treatment ​works to ​influence ‌compliance checks.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sandra Laville

item.title: ​World’s largest meat company faces legal challenge over green credentials of $6bn global expansion
item.description:
Greenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rights

The world’s biggest meat company, JBS, faces a landmark legal challenge to disclose how its $6bn (£4.5bn) global expansion plan is compatible with responsibilities to the climate, nature and human rights.

Courts in the Netherlands, where JBS is headquartered, will assess the petition filed by Greenpeace for information on whether the company is compliant with Dutch laws on “duty of care” as well as its own publicly stated commitments.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Watts

item.title: Trump must divulge financial details about businesses in $10bn BBC lawsuit
item.description:
US judge’s ruling could open up window into first family’s multiple ventures, in drawback of lawsuit on media

Donald Trump must divulge detailed financial information from his many businesses as part of his $10bn defamation lawsuit against the British Broadcasting Corporation, a federal judge ruled during a discovery hearing on Tuesday, according to news reports.

The ruling by US magistrate judge Enjoliqué Lett could open a unique window into the hundreds of businesses owned by Trump’s family trust. It also highlights a potential drawback for Trump’s strategy of bludgeoning critical media with multibillion-dollar lawsuits.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Roque Planas

item.title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert
item.description:
Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea

item.dc:creator: Raphael Rashid

item.title: House passes spending bill to head off government shutdown before midterms
item.description:
Measure authorizes spending until 4 December as Republicans seek to avoid volatile issue in election season

The US House of Representatives on Tuesday approved a stopgap measure to head off a government shutdown until early December, with its Republican leaders seeking to defuse a volatile issue in the run-up to the November midterm elections.

The continuing resolution authorizing government spending until 4 December passed by a vote of 220-205, roughly along party lines. It next heads to the Senate, where it will need to win bipartisan support to clear the chamber.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Chris Stein in Washington

item.title: ‘The explosion is what I remember’: man nearly sucked out of Ryanair plane recounts ordeal
item.description:
Exclusive: Ljubisa Karović tells Guardian the fact that he lived to tell the tale is nothing short of a miracle

It is the explosion just before he was nearly sucked out of the aircraft that Ljubisa Karović remembers. The thunderous noise of the window shattering that roused him from slumber in 11F, his window seat on the early morning Ryanair flight from Greece to Germany.

Eleven days and 11 nights later, it remains the prelude “to the chaos, the catastrophe” that keeps him awake at night.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Helena Smith in Katerini

item.title: ‘If all else fails, sue’: how ultra-processed food firms are using the courts to obstruct health rules
item.description:
Exclusive: Global investigation reveals 235 lawsuits have been brought against governments in five countries since 2010

The world’s biggest ultra-processed food companies have filed scores of lawsuits challenging government policies that tackle junk diets, the Guardian can reveal.

Ultra-processed food (UPF) is linked to harm in every major organ system of the human body and poses a seismic threat to global health. Worldwide consumption is rising and UPF now makes up as much as half the average diet in many countries, including the UK, the US and Australia.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Andrew Gregory, Thin Lei Win and Emmanuel Freudenthal

item.title: ‘Risking blackouts’? How Great Britain’s grid operator was dragged into a political row
item.description:
Neso denies allegations aired by Tory MP that staff took part in cover-up over decision-making in heatwave

In a control room in Wokingham, Berkshire, staff work around the clock, minute by minute, to align Great Britain’s fluctuating power supplies with its ever-changing demand.

However, as households struggle to adapt to soaring summer temperatures, staff at the government-owned National Energy System Operator (Neso), tasked with keeping the lights on, have faced the kind of challenge usually reserved for the country’s coldest winter nights in recent weeks.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jillian Ambrose Energy correspondent

item.title: ‘I fell in love with the art of coffee-making’: the female baristas shaking up Somalia’s booming cafe scene
item.description:
As fear and fighting ease in Mogadishu, coffee shops have become catalysts for change, bringing jobs for women and creating much-needed social spaces

Every morning at six, Saabiriin Hassan slips on her apron as she takes her place behind the counter at Cplus Cafe in the heart of Mogadishu. The aroma of freshly brewed coffee fills the air, but it is Hassan’s ever-present smile that draws the attention first.

It stays with her every move – from grinding beans and steaming milk to chatting with customers. Hassan is among the first female baristas in Somalia, serving coffee and helping a new cafe culture take root in a capital city once only known for conflict.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Moulid Hujale in Mogadishu

item.title: I joined the long lines for Trader Joe’s viral tote bags. What I found surprised me
item.description:
Shoppers are waking up before sunrise to score the $3 mini grocery bags, which have become a global status symbol

A Friday morning on a public holiday usually means sleeping in. Instead, I was standing outside my local Trader Joe’s before 7am, staring at a line of shoppers that seemed to stretch for miles.

The grocery store in Santa Clarita, just north of Los Angeles, wouldn’t open for another hour. But hundreds of people were already waiting for the same thing: Trader Joe’s new striped pastel mini canvas totes, a $2.99 reusable bag that has inspired a frenzy more typically associated with sneaker releases or concert presale tickets.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Paul Bersebach/Orange County Register via Getty Images
Photograph: Paul Bersebach/Orange County Register via Getty Images
Photograph: Paul Bersebach/Orange County Register via Getty Images

item.dc:creator: Liz Lindain in Santa Clarita, California

item.title: It cost £2.1bn to divert a river in Brazil’s outback. So where has all the water gone?
item.description:
Despite official claims that the São Francisco River project supplies 12m people, many of those who had to make way for its construction in 2007 still have no water at all

A concrete canal cutting through the Caatinga scrubland feeds a surface reservoir two and a half miles from Maria de Fátima Ferreira da Silva’s home in rural Brazil. The canal passes Sertânia and continues until it reaches Monteiro, the next town along, where it spills its precious cargo into the bed of the Paraíba River – a human-directed inflow into the seasonally dry waterway.

Such proximity to a reliable water source would usually be considered a blessing in the sertão, a vast outback in Brazil’s north-east known for its punishing droughts. But not for Ferreira, who was displaced nearly 20 years ago by the construction of the canal and now lives in a house without any water supply, despite the nearby reservoir.

Maria de Fátima Ferreira da Silva in her maize crop at Salão productive rural village. She bathes at the reservoir and collects water there for her smallholding and donkey, and pays to fill her barrel with drinking water each month

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Constance Malleret in Sertânia and Monteiro, Brazil. Photographs by Leonardo Carrato for the Guardian

item.title: ‘We were at their mercy’: inside the Amazon tactics that hiked prices across the internet
item.description:
Internal emails revealed in court and interviews with ex-employees describe how Amazon’s practices drove up prices for competitors like Walmart and Target. Amazon denies price-fixing claims and says it works to lower costs for consumers

A “modern leather” table lamp, listed online at Walmart, shot up from $24.99 to $39.

The price of an air fryer on a retail site called Newegg went from $84.99 to $149.99.

An electric ice-cream maker was listed at just $17.99 at Amazon and Best Buy. Then it became unavailable at Best Buy, and more than tripled in price to $59.99 at Amazon.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: George Joseph

item.title: ‘A guy did a runner in a Jaguar’: the petrol theft scandal – and the garage owner struggling to stop it
item.description:
Since the start of the Iran war in February, there has been a 23% rise in fuel theft, with more than £1.2m stolen from British forecourts each week. Why aren’t the police doing more to apprehend the culprits?

A petrol station tucked away in the suburbs of Birmingham seems an unlikely place for a crime spree. But this garage has become a prime target for thieves. Some fuel runners are easy to spot, approaching the petrol pumps in face masks, but after a recent spate of fill-and-dashes by less conspicuous drivers, no motorist is beyond suspicion. “It could be anyone,” says Shailesh Parekh, who is on high alert, as always.

The 62-year-old owns six forecourts across Birmingham, and today I’m visiting his station in Erdington, a residential area about five miles from the city centre. Parekh remembers a time when a motorist speeding off with an unpaid-for tank was rare. So much so, it would be the talk of the station for months. Those days are long gone. Now, it’s a weekly and sometimes daily occurrence. “In the last three years, it’s been very bad,” Parekh tells me from the back office, where a monitor beams CCTV images from every inch of the forecourt.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sammy Gecsoyler

item.title: ‘No electricity, no water, no fuel’: Ukrainian drone strikes bring darkness to Crimea’s summer
item.description:
Interviews with residents of Russian-held region point to frustration with Moscow as Ukrainian campaign to isolate it takes toll on daily life

First, the guests stopped coming. As Ukrainian drones and missiles pounded Crimea throughout the spring and into the summer, Russian tourists began cancelling their bookings at the small guesthouse Marina runs in Yevpatoria, a resort town on the peninsula’s Black Sea coast.

Then petrol became scarce, making it harder to stock the business with food and other essentials. Now, electricity and running water are cut off for much of the day, just as the summer heat sets in.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Pjotr Sauer

item.title: ‘Buy-cott’ boosts Memphis pizza shop after owner refuses to serve national guard
item.description:
The city rallied behind Tamboli’s despite online boycott calls, as frustration grows over the Trump administration’s federal taskforce following multiple deaths

You can measure the disconnect between angry internet commentariat and real life Memphis residents by the length of the wait for a table at Tamboli’s Pasta & Pizza.

In early July, Miles Tamboli, the owner of the midtown pizza spot, made a decision: he would not serve the national guard deployed to the city by the Trump administration, which the US president claimed would reduce the city’s crime rate. Tamboli’s video announcement came days after troops killed two city residents, bringing the total to four since the initiative started in September.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: George Chidi in Memphis

item.title: Andy Burnham to cut tax on electricity to ease cost of living - video
item.description:
The prime minister told his new cabinet in its first meeting that the government will be a 'cost-of-living government' and needs to examine 'all possible ways' to help families struggling with high costs. The new prime minister announced an £850m tax cut on electricity bills in his first significant policy announcement

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Source: Reuters

item.title: Andy Burnham pledges to address cost of living in first speech as UK PM - video
item.description:
The new UK prime minister, Andy Burnham, has given his first speech after accepting the king's invitation to form a government. In his speech, he pledged to bring in a 10-year plan for Britain as well as planning to end rough sleeping, 'build a new economy' and address the cost of living crisis. 'I can do something to give people some breathing space now. I will set out some of those measures tomorrow, including how we pay for them,' he said

Continue reading...

item.title: Burnham begins: what awaits the new PM? - podcast
item.description:
Senior political correspondent Peter Walker and former No 10 press secretary Paul Harrison analyse Andy Burnham’s first day as PM

On Monday, Andy Burnham finally realised his long-held ambition to become prime minister. But what problems can he expect to confront now he’s in No 10?

Former Downing Street press secretary Paul Harrison talks through the challenges of the office, from the awkward living arrangements to the even more awkward conversations with the president of the US.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Presented by Helen Pidd with Peter Walker and Paul Harrison; produced by Ruth Abrahams, Guy Szafman, Belinda Gurung and Rudi Zygadlo; executive producer Sami Kent

item.title: Can Andy Burnham’s cabinet fix Britain? – podcast
item.description:
The new prime minister has promised a ‘circuit breaker’ moment for the country, as he becomes the UK’s seventh leader since 2016. But will he be able to make the changes that are desperately needed? Pippa Crerar and Kiran Stacey look at how he will try to do things differently and who will be helping him in the cabinet

Please keep sending your comments and questions to Pippa and Kiran at politicsweeklyuk@theguardian.com

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Presented by Pippa Crerar and Kiran Stacey, produced by Sam Gruet and Nada Smiljanic, music by Axel Kacoutié, the executive producer is Frankie Tobi

item.title: Our streets are full of oversized cars | Brief letters
item.description:
Carspreading | An interregnum | Served by Thames Water | Manchesterism | Gransplaining | Cold in Yorkshire

Re “carspreading” (Opinion, 12 July), my wife and I live in a neighbourhood of houses built just before the second world war, and have watched in horror as models of our favoured cars have increased in size. Having chosen to keep our Mini Cooper and VW Polo – both of 2016 vintage – I believe we are the only people in the locality who store both cars in the garage overnight.
John Pye
Southampton

• A “period between reigns” (Quick crossword, 13 July) is an interregnum, not a regency as your solution has it. A regency occurs when a monarch is deemed incapable of carrying out his/her duties and they are taken over by a regent.
Peter Wrigley
Birstall, West Yorkshire

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff

item.title: British Gas mixed up our energy meter – but now I’m being penalised
item.description:
When the problem was finally sorted out in our new-build home, it increased my tariff by 19%

Residents in our new housing development are being billed for each other’s energy after the meters were mixed up.

I realised British Gas was sending bills for my energy to the developer of the estate instead of to me. I informed British Gas of my details when I moved in seven months ago.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Anna Tims

item.title: Why can’t Lifestyle & Mobility store show compassion after my loss?
item.description:
I bought a wheelchair for my daughter, but she later died in hospital and the company refuses a refund

I bought a £435 wheelchair for my disabled daughter from my local Lifestyle & Mobility store.

She had been admitted to hospital for tests and I planned to use the chair to take her on outings when she was discharged.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Anna Tims

item.title: Rogue locksmiths prey on people’s anxieties | Letters
item.description:
Chris Lohan was stung by a huge bill for a shoddy job. But Anna Bliss had a positive experience with a locksmith who helped her mum

I was the victim of a locksmith scam after searching Google and booking through the website advertised at the top of the page (The scary rise of locksmith scams: ‘I was shut out with my baby – and charged £2,200 to get back in’, 15 July). A broken lock resulted in a £490 bill. I stupidly paid the man, then I checked on Amazon for the price of the lock he fitted – £15.

I called the number advertised on Google to complain, but the chap who answered said he knew nothing about the guy who had come to our place. By then I was realising that he was involved – as well as denying accountability, they probably worked the scam together. The guy that came to mine also left a number, which I called, but it was ignored, and I eventually gave up.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Posed by model; Viktoriya Dikareva/Getty Images
Photograph: Posed by model; Viktoriya Dikareva/Getty Images
Photograph: Posed by model; Viktoriya Dikareva/Getty Images

item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff
